skill.name: asana-automation
skill.description: Automate Asana work through Rube MCP, including tasks, projects, sections, teams, users, and workspace discovery.
skill.summary: Manage Asana tasks, projects, sections, teams, and users through Rube MCP tool workflows.
skill.author: sickn33

security_audit.summary: Static command-execution, weak-crypto, and reconnaissance findings are false positives caused by markdown tool names, parameters, and Asana API concepts. The confirmed risk is that this community skill directs users to connect a third-party MCP endpoint with OAuth access to Asana and then perform write-capable operations.

medium_findings.title: Third-Party MCP Endpoint Handles Asana OAuth Access
medium_findings.description: The skill instructs users to add the Rube MCP endpoint and connect Asana through OAuth. This is expected functionality, but it gives a third-party MCP integration access to Asana workspace data and actions.
medium_findings.confidence_reasoning: The external endpoint and OAuth connection flow are explicitly documented. The risk is contextual because third-party access is intended, not hidden.

medium_findings.title: Write-Capable Asana Operations Can Modify Workspace Data
medium_findings.description: The workflow lists task, project, section, and subtask creation or movement tools. These operations can change production Asana data if a user authorizes the integration.
medium_findings.confidence_reasoning: The documented tools include create and move actions. This is a real operational risk, but it matches the stated Asana automation purpose.

low_findings.title: False Positive Command Execution From Markdown Backticks
low_findings.description: The static analyzer treated inline markdown code spans as Ruby or shell backtick execution. The lines contain Rube and Asana tool names, parameter names, and documentation examples, not executable code.
low_findings.confidence_reasoning: The file is markdown documentation and contains no Ruby, shell script, or command substitution. The backticks only format tool and parameter names.

low_findings.title: False Positive Weak Cryptography Detection
low_findings.description: The reported lines do not implement or call cryptographic algorithms. They are the frontmatter description and a task notes parameter description.
low_findings.confidence_reasoning: No cryptographic operation is present on the cited lines. The finding appears to be triggered by unrelated text substrings.

low_findings.title: False Positive System Reconnaissance Detection
low_findings.description: The reported lines refer to Asana workspaces, users, teams, GIDs, and task resources. They do not inspect the host operating system or collect local system information.
low_findings.confidence_reasoning: The context is Asana API resource lookup. No local hostname, user, process, network, or filesystem reconnaissance command is documented.

dangerous_patterns.title: External MCP Server Configuration
dangerous_patterns.description: The skill directs users to configure an external MCP server. Users should understand that Asana data and actions may flow through that integration.
dangerous_patterns.confidence_reasoning: The hardcoded Rube MCP endpoint is explicit. It is expected for the skill, but it is still an external network dependency.

dangerous_patterns.title: Bulk Parallel API Requests
dangerous_patterns.description: The skill includes a parallel request tool and notes that failed requests do not roll back successful ones. Bulk actions can create partial updates.
dangerous_patterns.confidence_reasoning: The documentation explicitly describes parallel API calls and lack of rollback. The risk depends on user-selected actions.

content.value_statement: Asana automation can require many tool lookups and repeated ID resolution. This skill guides Claude, Codex, and Claude Code through reliable Rube MCP workflows.
actual_capabilities: Find Asana workspaces before running workspace-scoped operations.
Create, search, list, and inspect Asana tasks and subtasks.
Create projects and sections, then move tasks into sections.
List teams, team members, workspace users, and the authenticated user.
Use Rube tool search before selecting current Asana tool schemas.
Run parallel Asana API actions when bulk operations are needed.
limitations: Requires Rube MCP and an active Asana OAuth connection.
Depends on current Rube and Asana tool schemas at runtime.
Cannot guarantee rollback for parallel requests that partially fail.
Does not define approval gates for destructive or high-volume changes.

use_cases.title: Create Tasks From Meeting Follow-Ups
use_cases.description: Turn action items into Asana tasks with owners, due dates, projects, and subtasks.

use_cases.title: Organize Project Sections
use_cases.description: Create sections and move existing tasks into the right workflow stages.

use_cases.title: Audit Team Membership
use_cases.description: List workspace teams, team members, and users before assigning tasks or projects.

prompt_templates.title: List My Asana Workspaces
prompt_templates.prompt: Use the Asana automation skill to find my available workspaces. Show each workspace name and GID before doing anything else.

prompt_templates.title: Create A Project Task
prompt_templates.prompt: Find the correct workspace and project, then create an Asana task named [task name] assigned to [person] due on [date].

prompt_templates.title: Organize Tasks Into Sections
prompt_templates.prompt: In project [project name], find tasks matching [criteria], list available sections, and move each task into the correct section after confirmation.

prompt_templates.title: Plan A Bulk Update
prompt_templates.prompt: Prepare a parallel Asana update plan for [bulk change]. Show the actions, expected effects, and rollback risks before submitting requests.

output_examples.input: Create follow-up tasks for the launch checklist in the Marketing workspace.
output: Identifies the Marketing workspace and the target project.
Creates named tasks with due dates, assignees, and project placement.
Returns task links and any unresolved assignee or project issues.

output_examples.input: Move completed design tasks into the Done section.
output: Lists matching design tasks and confirms the Done section GID.
Moves approved tasks into the section.
Reports which tasks moved and which tasks need manual review.

output_examples.input: Show team members before assigning project owners.
output: Finds the workspace team and lists available members.
Shows names and identifiers needed for assignment.
Avoids task assignment until the target owner is confirmed.

best_practices: Search Rube tools first so the workflow uses current tool schemas.
Resolve workspace, project, section, and task GIDs before write actions.
Ask for confirmation before bulk updates, moves, or project creation.
anti_patterns: Do not assume project names are unique across workspaces.
Do not run parallel requests before reviewing partial failure impact.
Do not create or move tasks without confirming the target workspace.

faq.question: Does this skill require an Asana account?
faq.answer: Yes. It requires an active Asana connection through Rube MCP before workflows can run.

faq.question: Can it create and update Asana tasks?
faq.answer: Yes. The documented workflows include task creation, subtasks, section moves, and project organization.

faq.question: Why does it search tools first?
faq.answer: Rube tool schemas can change. Searching first helps the assistant use current parameters and avoid stale calls.

faq.question: Can it work across multiple workspaces?
faq.answer: Yes, but it should resolve the correct workspace GID before each workspace-scoped operation.

faq.question: Is bulk automation supported?
faq.answer: Yes. The skill documents parallel requests, but users should review partial failure risks first.

faq.question: Is this safe for production Asana workspaces?
faq.answer: Use it with review steps. It can make real changes through an authorized third-party MCP integration.
